prompt: Which work in the corpus opens with a deity who, after rescuing the Earth from the cosmic waters in the form of a Boar whose tusks are the sacrificial post and tongue is the sacrificial fire, vanishes — prompting the Earth-goddess to take the form of a beautiful woman with lips red as the Bandhujīva flower, seek out the sage Kashyapa, and at his direction travel to the Ocean of Milk to ask the reclining god 'what will be my support?' before being seated on a golden throne to receive the law? Quote the relevant verses and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This Vaiṣṇava cosmogonic frame — the Boar-rescue followed by the Earth taking female form, the Kashyapa intermediary, the journey to the Ocean of Milk to find the god on Śeṣa, and the goddess seated on a golden throne to receive dharma — is the framing device of this smṛti and is found in no other dharmaśāstra in the corpus (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Kātyāyana, Bṛhaspati, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa, and Yama all use other framings).

prompt: Which work in the corpus enumerates a sequence of named hells — beginning with Tāmisra (Darkness) and Andhatāmisra (Blinding Darkness), passing through Raurava (Howling) and Mahāraurava (Great Howling), Kālasūtra (the Black Thread), Saṃjīvana (Reviving), Avīci (Waveless), Kuḍmala (Bud), Pūtimṛttika (Putrid Clay), Lohaśaṅku (Iron Spike), Kaṇṭakaśālmali (Thorny Silk-Cotton Tree), Asipatravana (Forest of Sword-leaves), and ending at Lohacāraka (Iron Cage) — and assigns durations of a Kalpa for heinous sinners and a Manvantara for great sinners? Quote the named-hell list and the duration verses and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: While other smṛtis in the corpus speak generically of 'hell' or 'narakas', only this work supplies a sequential roster of twenty-two named hells with this specific ordering and assigns Kalpa/Manvantara/Catur-yuga durations to the sin classes; Manu's transmigration chapter does not name the hells in this catalog form.

prompt: Which work in the corpus closes by having the deity address the Earth-goddess by name — 'O Dhara' — and praise the law-treatise just delivered as 'spoken by the god himself,' calling it a 'destroyer of bad dreams,' an 'abode of auspiciousness,' to be recited at śrāddhas by men who desire prosperity? Quote the closing verses verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The closing benediction in which the speaker is the god himself and the addressee is the Earth-goddess Dhara — combined with the unusual self-praise as a 'destroyer of bad dreams' and the prescription that the text be recited at śrāddhas — is uniquely the closing chapter of this smṛti's frame-tale; no other dharmaśāstra in the corpus closes with a divine first-person address to Pṛthivī.
